text:
The Applewood Center in Winchester hosted its 19th annual Senior Olympics event on Thursday, Sept. 5.

More than 20 teams from nursing homes in New Hampshire and Vermont competed in person and virtually in five events.

The opening remarks were given by Luca Paris, President and CEO of Greater Monadnock Collaborative, who expressed gratitude toward the competitors for the examples they set for the younger generations. Ruth Cogswell, Applewood’s Director of Activities, sang the national anthem and a benediction was given by Pastor Stacey Hartford. The “Flame of Life” torch was passed along a line made up of the oldest residents from each team. Frank, a 101-year-old World War II veteran from Thompson House in Brattleboro was the oldest participant and had the honor of lighting the large Senior Olympic game torch.

There were five events held this year. Cornhole was won by David from Alpine Center in Keene, silver was won by Phyllis at Applewood in Winchester, and Maria from Vernon Green in Vernon, Vt., took the bronze. Virtual winners were Donna from Pine Heights (gold), Paul from Hackett Hill (silver) and Jeff from Pine Heights (bronze).

The horseshoes event was won by Jeff at Elmwood, Ida from Langdon Place of Nashua won silver, and Phyllis from Applewood took the bronze medal. Virtual winners were Larry from Pine Heights (gold), Sarah from Rochester Manor (silver) and Jeff from Pine Heights (bronze).

Wheelchair Slalom was won by Joe from Oceanside in Hampton (who was the bronze medal winner last year), George from Bedford Hills in Bedford was the silver medal winner, and John from Pleasant Valley in Derry was the bronze medalist. Virtual winners were Bill R. from Pheasant Wood (gold), Gordon from Pheasant Wood (silver) and Danielle from Ridgewood.

Bowling was won by Leo from Maplewood in Westmoreland. Larry from Applewood won the silver medal, and Paula from Maplewood was the bronze winner. Virtual winners were Steve from Rochester Manor (gold), Carl from Ridgewood (silver) and Donna from Crestwood (bronze).

Name That Tune is played by a two player team. Sharon and John from Bedford Hills in Bedford won the gold medal. Donna and Mary from Pleasant Valley in Derry won the silver, and David and Tina from Alpine Center in Keene won the bronze medal. The virtual team from Pine Heights won the gold, Vernon Green won the silver and Hackett Hill took the bronze medal.

Closing remarks were given by Brendan Williams the president and CEO of New Hampshire Health Care Association. He also helped Applewood Administrator Gail Cushing to distribute medals to the winners.
